The **Eighth Schedule** to the [Constitution of India](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India") lists the [languages](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India") officially recognized by the [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India"). As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India&action=edit), 22 languages have been classified under the schedule.

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

As per the [Constitution of India](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India"), the provisions belonging to the eight schedule are defined in articles 344(1) and 351. Article 344(1) defined a set of 14 regional languages which were represented in the [Official Languages Commission](/wiki/Official_Languages_Commission "Official Languages Commission") and Article 351 deals with the promotion of usage of [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") by [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India"), which was declared as an official language. [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") was declared as an additional official language to be used for a period not exceeding 15 years. The commission was to suggest steps to be taken to progressively promote the use of Hindi as the official language of the country.[[1]](#cite_note-Constitution-1)

## Official Languages Act, 1963

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Official Languages Act, 1963")]

Main article: [Official Languages Act, 1963](/wiki/Official_Languages_Act,_1963 "Official Languages Act, 1963")

The Official Languages Act, 1963 which came into effect on 26 January 1965, made provisions for the continuation of English as an official language alongside Hindi in official communications after the expiration of the fifteen year period.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

On 18 January 1968, the Official Language Resolution was passed by the [Parliament of India](/wiki/Parliament_of_India "Parliament of India") which further mandated the development and promotion of all the languages listed in the Eighth Schedule. As per the resolution, the Government of India was obligated to take measures for the development of the languages defined in the eighth schedule.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

### Initial list (1950)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Initial list (1950)")]

The original Eighth Schedule in 1950 included 14 languages:

* [Assamese](/wiki/Assamese_language "Assamese language")
* [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language")
* [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language")
* [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi")
* [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada")
* [Kashmiri](/wiki/Kashmiri_language "Kashmiri language")
* [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam")
* [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language")
* [Odia](/wiki/Odia_language "Odia language")[[a]](#cite_note-6)
* [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language")
* [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit")
* [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language")
* [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language")
* [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu")

### Additions through constitutional amendments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Additions through constitutional amendments")]

As of 2026, subsequent amendments have added eight additional languages:[[6]](#cite_note-7)

* 21st Amendment (1967): [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language") was added as the 15th language.
* 71st Amendment (1992): [Konkani](/wiki/Konkani_language "Konkani language"), [Manipuri](/wiki/Manipuri_language "Manipuri language"), and [Nepali](/wiki/Nepali_language "Nepali language") were included, increasing the total to 18 languages.
* 92nd Amendment (2003): Four languages: [Bodo](/wiki/Boro_language_(India) "Boro language (India)"), [Dogri](/wiki/Dogri_language "Dogri language"), [Santali](/wiki/Santali_language "Santali language"), and [Maithili](/wiki/Maithili_language "Maithili language"), were added, raising the total number to 22 languages.
* 96th Amendment (2011): Oriya was renamed Odia.

## Scheduled languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Scheduled languages")]

The Eighth Schedule of the Constitution defined 14 languages in 1950:[[7]](#cite_note-8) [Assamese](/wiki/Assamese_language "Assamese language"), [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language"), [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"), [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada"), [Kashmiri](/wiki/Kashmiri_language "Kashmiri language"), [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"), [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language"), [Odia](/wiki/Odia_language "Odia language"), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"), [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu").[[8]](#cite_note-Eight-9)
In 1967, the [21st amendment](/wiki/Twenty-first_Amendment_of_the_Constitution_of_India "Twenty-first Amendment of the Constitution of India") to the constitution added [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language") to the Eighth Schedule.
The [71st Amendment](/wiki/Seventy-first_Amendment_of_the_Constitution_of_India "Seventy-first Amendment of the Constitution of India"), enacted in 1992, added three more languages: [Konkani](/wiki/Konkani_language "Konkani language"), [Manipuri](/wiki/Meitei_language "Meitei language") and [Nepali](/wiki/Nepali_language "Nepali language").
In 2003, the [92nd Amendment](/wiki/Ninety-second_Amendment_of_the_Constitution_of_India "Ninety-second Amendment of the Constitution of India") added [Bodo](/wiki/Bodo_language "Bodo language"), [Dogri](/wiki/Dogri_language "Dogri language"), [Santali](/wiki/Santali_language "Santali language") and [Maithili](/wiki/Maithili_language "Maithili language"), raising the total number of languages to 22.[[8]](#cite_note-Eight-9)
In 2011, the spelling Oriya was changed to Odia by 96th amendment.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

## Demands for expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Demands for expansion")]

In 2003, a committee was established by [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India"), to study the possible inclusion of more languages to the schedule. As per the [Ministry of Home Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Home_Affairs_(India) "Ministry of Home Affairs (India)"), there are demands for inclusion of 38 more languages in the Eighth Schedule to the Constitution. ## Arguments regarding expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Arguments regarding expansion")]

### Arguments for inclusion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Arguments for inclusion")]

* Cultural preservation: Many communities argue that inclusion in the Eighth Schedule would help preserve and promote their [cultural heritage](/wiki/Cultural_heritage "Cultural heritage") and linguistic identity.[[16]](#cite_note-17)
* Administrative recognition: Inclusion grants official recognition, ensuring the use of these languages in government administration and education.
* Increased resources: Inclusion allows for funding and resources to be allocated for the development and promotion of the language.

### Arguments against inclusion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Arguments against inclusion")]

* Administrative complexity: Recognizing too many languages could complicate governance, translation, and communication.
* Dilution of resources: Increased inclusion could divert resources and attention from the development of existing languages.
* Politicization of language: Inclusion may encourage regional and political pressures, leading to disputes over linguistic priorities.
